title: Covillea glutinosa
intro: Covillea glutinosa, also known as sticky globemallow, is a perennial plant species native to the southwestern United States and Mexico. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, offering potential health benefits in the treatment of wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Covillea glutinosa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Covillea glutinosa is often used in herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as in topical creams and salves for its wound-healing and anti-inflammatory properties.
taxonomy: Covillea glutinosa is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Cucurbitaceae, commonly known as the cucumber or gourd family. This classification places Covillea glutinosa within the larger group of flowering plants, specifically the dicotyledons. Further classification within the family Cucurbitaceae is still being researched, but it is likely to be part of a larger genus or subfamily.
